label: honzo_wamyo::daio_rhubarb
text: 大黄 (Daio / Da Huang) — Rheum palmatum / R. officinale. Honzo Wamyo entry: 大黄、和名 加波良久佐。性寒、味苦。主下瘀血、血閉、寒熱、破癥瘕積聚、留飲宿食、蕩滌腸胃、推陳致新、通利水穀、調中化食、安和五臓。Daio, Japanese name Kawarakusa. Nature: cold. Taste: bitter. Primary uses: dispels static blood, opens blocked blood, treats alternating cold and heat, breaks up accumulations and masses, dissolves retained fluids and old food, flushes and purges the intestines and stomach, eliminates the old and generates the new, facilitates passage of water and grain, regulates the middle and transforms food, calms and harmonises the five viscera. Preparation: root decocted 3-12g; reduce cooking time to preserve cathartic effect. Kampo use: Daio-kanzoto formula (rhubarb + licorice) for constipation. Contraindicated in pregnancy and weakness.

label: honzo_wamyo::kanzo_licorice
text: 甘草 (Kanzo / Gan Cao) — Glycyrrhiza uralensis. Honzo Wamyo: 甘草、和名 阿末加都良。甘、平。主五臓六腑寒熱邪気、堅筋骨、長肌肉、倍力、金創腫、解毒。久服輕身延年。Kanzo, Japanese name Amakatsura. Sweet, neutral. Indicated for cold and heat pathogenic factors in the five viscera and six bowels, strengthens sinews and bones, builds flesh and muscle, increases strength, for metal-wound swelling, antidotes poisons. Long-term use lightens the body and extends life. The most frequently used herb in all of Kampo — present in approximately 70% of compound formulas as a harmoniser and moderator of other herbs' actions.

label: honzo_wamyo::keishi_cinnamon
text: 桂枝 (Keishi / Gui Zhi) — Cinnamomum cassia (young twigs). Honzo Wamyo: 桂枝、和名 加爾。辛、温。主上気咳逆、結気喉痺吐吸、利関節、補中益気。Keishi, Japanese name Kani. Pungent, warm. Primary uses: descends rebellious qi, stops coughing, opens constricted qi in throat, facilitates movement in the joints, tonifies the middle and benefits qi. Yoshimasu Todo (18th c. Koho school): Keishi is the primary herb for dispelling cold from the surface and warming the channels. Keishi-bukuryo-gan formula (cinnamon + hoelen fungus): documented Kampo treatment for pelvic inflammatory conditions and menstrual disorders with multiple modern clinical trials.

label: honzo_wamyo::mao_ephedra
text: 麻黄 (Mao / Ma Huang) — Ephedra sinica. Honzo Wamyo: 麻黄、和名 宇末乃久佐。苦、温。主中風傷寒頭痛、温瘧、發表出汗、去邪熱気、止咳逆上気、除寒熱、破癥堅積聚。Mao (Ephedra), Japanese name Umanokusa. Bitter, warm. Indicated for wind-cold with headache, warm malaria, promotes sweating to dispel exterior pathogens, clears fever-heat, stops coughing and rebellious qi, dispels alternating cold and heat, breaks up hard accumulations. Kampo clinical refinement: Yoshimasu Todo noted that Japanese patients required lower doses than Chinese classical texts specified — the dosing was refined empirically over centuries. Ephedrine isolated from ma huang by Nagai Nagayoshi 1885.

label: honzo_wamyo::oren_coptis
text: 黄連 (Oren / Huang Lian) — Coptis japonica / C. chinensis. Honzo Wamyo: 黄連、和名 加加美久佐。苦、寒。主熱気目痛、眥傷泣出、明目、腸澼腹痛下痢、婦人陰中腫痛。Oren, Japanese name Kakamikusa. Bitter, cold. Primary: heat conditions with eye pain and lacrimation, brightens vision, intestinal flux with abdominal pain and diarrhea, women's internal swelling and pain. Active compound: berberine (ベルベリン) — antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, blood glucose regulation. Oren-gedoku-to formula: widely used in Japanese medicine for inflammatory conditions. Modern research: berberine shows clinical evidence comparable to metformin for blood glucose management in Type 2 diabetes.

label: honzo_wamyo::bofu_siler
text: 防風 (Bofu / Fang Feng) — Saposhnikovia divaricata. Honzo Wamyo entry documenting this as a major Kampo herb for wind-type conditions. 防風、和名 加是久佐。甘、温。主大風頭眩痛、悪風風邪、目盲無所見、風行周身骨節疼痺煩満。Bofu (Siler), Japanese name Kazekusa (wind-grass). Sweet, warm. Indicated for major wind disease with head dizziness and pain, aversion to wind, wind pathogenic factors, blindness with inability to see, wind travelling through the entire body causing bone joint pain and obstruction with a feeling of fullness. Major Kampo formula: Bofu-tsusho-san — one of the most prescribed Kampo formulas in modern Japan, used for obesity, metabolic syndrome, and constipation. Multiple RCTs conducted.

label: honzo_wamyo::shakunyaku_peony
text: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku / Shao Yao) — Paeonia lactiflora. Honzo Wamyo: 芍薬、和名 恵比須久佐。苦、平。主邪気腹痛、除血痺、破堅積、寒熱疝瘕、止痛、利小便、益気。Shakuyaku, Japanese name Ebisukusa. Bitter, neutral. Main uses: pathogenic factor causing abdominal pain, dispels blood obstruction, breaks up hard accumulations, alternating cold and heat with shan conglomeration, stops pain, promotes urination, benefits qi. Active compound: paeoniflorin — muscle relaxant, anti-spasmodic, anti-inflammatory. Shakuyaku-kanzo-to (peony + licorice): remarkable anti-spasmodic formula, RCT evidence for muscle cramps and dysmenorrhea. One of most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas.

label: honzo_wamyo::bukuryo_poria
text: 茯苓 (Bukuryo / Fu Ling) — Poria cocos (sclerotium of fungus growing on pine roots). Honzo Wamyo: 茯苓、和名 末都保止。甘、平。主胸脇逆気、憂恚驚邪恐悸、心下結痛、寒熱煩満咳逆、口焦舌乾、利小便。Bukuryo, Japanese name Matsuhoji (pine-holding). Sweet, neutral. Indicated for rebellious qi in the chest and ribs, worry, anger, fright, pathogenic factors, fearfulness and palpitations, painful binding below the heart, alternating cold and heat with irritability and fullness, cough with rebellious qi, dry mouth and tongue, promotes urination. Widely used Kampo sedative-calming agent. Active: pachymic acid, beta-glucans — immune modulating, anxiolytic. Keishi-bukuryo-gan: one of most clinically studied Kampo formulas, evidence for uterine fibroids, endometriosis.

label: honzo_wamyo::saikou_bupleurum
text: 柴胡 (Saikou / Chai Hu) — Bupleurum falcatum. Honzo Wamyo: 柴胡、和名 美之末。苦、平。主心腹腸胃中結気、飲食積聚、寒熱邪気、推陳致新。久服輕身明目益精。Saikou (Thorowax), Japanese name Mishima. Bitter, neutral. Main: constricted qi in the heart, abdomen, intestines and stomach, accumulation of food and drink, alternating cold and heat from pathogenic factors, eliminates the old and promotes the new. Long-term use lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and replenishes essence. Sho-saiko-to (Minor Bupleurum Decoction): extensively studied in Japan for chronic hepatitis. Multiple clinical trials showing hepatoprotective effects. Mechanism: saikosaponins modulate immune response and reduce hepatic inflammation.

label: honzo_wamyo::ougi_astragalus
text: 黄耆 (Ougi / Huang Qi) — Astragalus membranaceus. Honzo Wamyo: 黄耆、和名 木乃美奈。甘、微温。主癰疽久敗瘡、排膿止痛、大風癩疾、五痔鼠瘻、補虚小児百病。Ougi, Japanese name Konomi-na. Sweet, mildly warm. Indicated for carbuncles and deep abscesses with chronic festering sores, promotes pus drainage and stops pain, serious wind disease and leprosy, five types of hemorrhoids, mouse-track sores, supplements deficiency, and all children's diseases. Modern Kampo: major immune-tonifying herb. Active: astragalosides, polysaccharides. Active research on astragalus for immune modulation, cancer adjunct therapy, anti-aging (telomere effects). Astragaloside IV studied for cardiac protection post-myocardial infarction.

label: honzo_wamyo::shimotsu_to_formula
text: 四物湯 (Shimotsu-to / Si Wu Tang) — Four Substance Decoction. One of the foundational blood-tonifying formulas in Kampo. Components: 当帰 (Toki/Dang Gui, Angelica sinensis) 12g + 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Shao Yao, Paeonia lactiflora) 12g + 川芎 (Senkyu/Chuan Xiong, Ligusticum chuanxiong) 8g + 地黄 (Jio/Di Huang, Rehmannia glutinosa) 16g. Yoshimasu Todo on Shimotsu-to: This formula addresses blood deficiency patterns manifesting as pale complexion, dizziness, palpitations, menstrual irregularity, and dry skin. It was refined in Japan to use different proportions than the original Chinese formula based on clinical observation. The Koho school's emphasis on direct clinical observation and formula modification is documented in Yoshimasu Todo's Yakucho and Ruijuho. Modern research on individual components: Angelica sinensis (ferulic acid) — anti-platelet, blood-moving; Rehmannia — blood sugar regulation; Ligusticum — vasodilatory effects studied.

label: honzo_wamyo::hachimi_jio_gan
text: 八味地黄丸 (Hachimi-jio-gan) — Eight-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill. Foundational Kampo kidney-tonifying formula. Components: 地黄 (Rehmannia) 6g + 山薬 (Dioscoria) 3g + 山茱萸 (Cornus fruit) 3g + 茯苓 (Poria) 3g + 沢瀉 (Alisma) 3g + 牡丹皮 (Moutan bark) 3g + 桂枝 (Cinnamon twig) 1g + 附子 (Aconite, processed) 1g. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) as adaptation of Chinese classical formula. Modern Kampo use: frequent urination, lower back weakness, cold extremities, age-related decline in men. Clinical trials for: overactive bladder (positive RCT evidence), age-related cognitive decline, diabetic neuropathy. One of the most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas in Japan's national health insurance system. Annual prescriptions in Japan: millions of defined daily doses.

label: honzo_wamyo::toki_angelica
text: 当帰 (Toki / Dang Gui) — Angelica acutiloba (Japanese species distinct from Chinese A. sinensis). Honzo Wamyo: 当帰、和名 也末止己呂。甘辛、温。主咳逆上気、温瘧寒熱洒洒在皮膚中、婦人漏下絶子、諸悪瘡瘍、金創。Toki, Japanese name Yamatorokoro. Sweet-pungent, warm. Primary: cough with rebellious qi, warm malaria with alternating cold and heat in the skin, women's spotting (uterine bleeding) with infertility, all malign sores and ulcers, metal wounds. Note: Japanese Kampo uses A. acutiloba rather than the Chinese A. sinensis — a deliberate botanical distinction developed during Japan's Edo period isolation, reflecting Japanese clinical observation that the local species had different properties. Active compounds: ferulic acid, Z-ligustilide, phthalides. Major gynecological herb in modern Kampo.

label: honzo_wamyo::boiogito_formula
text: 防已黄耆湯 (Boi-ogi-to) — Stephania and Astragalus Decoction. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) and standard Kampo formulary. Components: 防已 (Boii/Stephania tetrandra) 5g + 黄耆 (Ougi/Astragalus) 5g + 白术 (Byakujutsu/Atractylodes) 3g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Glycyrrhiza) 1.5g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Zingiber) 1g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 3g. Traditional indications: sweating, heaviness, joint swelling particularly in the knees, knee osteoarthritis. Modern clinical evidence: multiple Japanese RCTs for knee osteoarthritis showing significant symptom reduction. One of the best-studied Kampo formulas for musculoskeletal conditions. Mechanism: stephania tetrandrine (anti-inflammatory alkaloid) + astragalus immune modulation + atractylodes diuretic effect for joint fluid accumulation.

label: honzo_wamyo::ninjin_ginseng
text: 人参 (Ninjin / Ren Shen) — Panax ginseng. Honzo Wamyo: 人参、和名 久留万久佐。甘、微寒。主補五臓、安精神、定魂魄、止驚悸、除邪気、明目開心益智。久服軽身延年。Ninjin, Japanese name Kurumakusa. Sweet, mildly cold. Primary: tonifies the five viscera, calms the spirit, stabilises the corporeal and ethereal souls, stops fright and palpitations, eliminates pathogenic factors, brightens the eyes, opens the heart, and benefits wisdom. Long-term use lightens the body and extends life. Kampo applications: major qi-tonifying herb, adapted dose (smaller than Chinese tradition) and combined with warming herbs to moderate its cold nature. Active: ginsenosides Rb1, Rg1, Re — adaptogenic, neuroprotective, anti-fatigue. Over 3,000 modern research papers. Clinical evidence for fatigue, immune modulation, cognitive function in elderly.

label: honzo_wamyo::shoyakukanzo_formula
text: 芍薬甘草湯 (Shakuyaku-kanzo-to) — Peony and Licorice Decoction. One of the most prescribed Kampo formulas in modern Japan. Components: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Paeonia) 20g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Glycyrrhiza) 20g. Documented in Jinkikyo (Jin Gui Yao Lue, classical Chinese), adapted in Japanese Kampo with higher doses than Chinese tradition. Traditional indications: muscle spasms, cramping, abdominal pain, leg cramps. Modern clinical applications with RCT evidence: (1) muscle cramps in hemodialysis patients — multiple Japanese RCTs showing significant reduction in cramp frequency; (2) dysmenorrhea — multiple RCTs showing comparable efficacy to NSAIDs; (3) PCOS-related androgen excess — several trials showing reduction in testosterone levels through paeoniflorin's anti-androgen mechanism; (4) chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy — used in Japanese oncology. One of the most evidence-rich Kampo formulas.

label: honzo_wamyo::shosaikoto_formula
text: 小柴胡湯 (Sho-saiko-to) — Minor Bupleurum Decoction. Classical Kampo formula documented from earliest Japanese adaptation of Chinese medicine. Components: 柴胡 (Saikou/Bupleurum) 7g + 半夏 (Hange/Pinellia) 5g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Ginger) 4g + 黄芩 (Ogon/Scutellaria) 3g + 人参 (Ninjin/Ginseng) 3g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Licorice) 2g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 3g. Traditional indications: alternating fever and chills, bitter taste in mouth, dry throat, nausea, chest and hypochondriac fullness. Modern applications: extensively studied in Japan for chronic hepatitis B and C — multiple large RCTs showing hepatoprotective effects, reduction of hepatitis B surface antigen levels, and reduction in liver cancer risk in cirrhotic patients. Mechanism: saikosaponins modulate NF-kB inflammatory pathway, Scutellaria baicalensis (baicalin) — antiviral and anti-inflammatory. Contraindication discovered 1994: should not be combined with interferon — severe interstitial pneumonia risk. This contraindication discovery from post-marketing surveillance in Japan is a model for traditional medicine pharmacovigilance.

label: honzo_wamyo::kakkonto_formula
text: 葛根湯 (Kakkon-to) — Kudzu Combination Decoction. Most widely recognised Kampo formula in Japan. Components: 葛根 (Kakkon/Pueraria lobata root) 8g + 麻黄 (Mao/Ephedra) 4g + 桂枝 (Keishi/Cinnamon) 3g + 芍薬 (Shakuyaku/Peony) 3g + 大棗 (Taiso/Jujube) 4g + 生姜 (Shokyo/Ginger) 4g + 甘草 (Kanzo/Licorice) 2g. Traditional indications: early stage cold or flu with stiffness of the back of the neck and upper back, fever without sweating, aversion to cold. Documented in Ishinho (984 CE) and standard Kampo practice throughout Japanese history. Modern applications: most frequently prescribed Kampo formula for common cold. Over-the-counter availability in Japan. Active in early viral infection stage. Puerarin (from kudzu root): vasodilatory, anti-platelet effects studied for cardiovascular applications. Used in Japanese emergency medicine for early-stage fever. Multiple observational studies showing shorter duration of common cold symptoms.

label: honzo_wamyo::sanshishi_gardenia
text: 山梔子 (Sanshishi / Zhi Zi) — Gardenia jasminoides fruit. Honzo Wamyo: 山梔子、和名 久知奈之。苦、寒。主五内邪気、胃中熱気、面赤酒皰皶鼻、白癩赤癩瘡瘍。Sanshishi, Japanese name Kuchinashi. Bitter, cold. Indicated for pathogenic qi in the five viscera, heat in the stomach, red face and rosacea, white and red leprosy, sores and ulcers. Major Kampo use: heat-clearing herb for inflammatory conditions. Active compound: genipin — extensively studied for: neuroprotection (anti-inflammatory in CNS), liver protection, treatment of depression (in animal models), anti-diabetic activity. Cross-tradition connection: gardenia used in both Chinese and Kampo traditions with identical indications — provides within-East-Asian convergence signal.

label: honzo_wamyo::byakujutsu_atractylodes
text: 白术 (Byakujutsu / Bai Zhu) — Atractylodes japonica (Japanese species) / A. macrocephala (Chinese species). Kampo uses Japanese species — distinct from Chinese use, representing independent clinical development. Honzo Wamyo: 白术、和名 於介良。苦、温。主風寒湿痺死肌、痙疸、止汗除熱、消食。Byakujutsu, Japanese name Okera. Bitter, warm. Main: wind-cold-damp obstruction with insensate flesh, spasms and jaundice, stops sweating, eliminates heat, promotes food digestion. The Japanese Kampo preference for A. japonica over A. macrocephala developed during the Edo period of isolation and is documented by Koho school physicians as reflecting genuinely different clinical properties. Active: atractylodin, beta-eudesmol — anti-inflammatory, gastroprotective, immune-modulating. Major digestive herb in Kampo formulas.
